posts.text:
Do the rich pay taxes? In the US, they do. And a lot of them.  

America has the most progressive tax system in the developed world, where high earners pay the largest share.  

If spending keeps rising, taxes will too.  The only solution is lower spending, says Cato’s @adamnmichel.

Video

The Trump administration should never have imposed tariffs under IEEPA. As Cato scholars now explain, the least the US government can do now is to quickly issue blanket tariff refunds.

ow.ly/Urfw50YtOU8

Published 250 years ago this month, “The Wealth of Nations” by Adam Smith remains among the most influential economics books ever written—laying the intellectual foundation for free markets, capitalism, and the classical liberal and libertarian traditions.

On “affordability,” what really matters is how many hours of work it takes to buy things. Many goods are affordable because wages have risen faster than prices, while housing, health care, and education cost more--largely due to policy-driven constraints, explains Cato’s @Marian_L_Tupy. ow.ly/rczu50Yt1FU

Section 230 sets simple principles that allow the law to adapt as technology evolves, says Sen. Ron Wyden. In this discussion, the Section 230 co-author reflects on how the statute continues to support online speech in the next generation of internet services.

Video

“It’s not us Americans versus them immigrants. Cities know immigrants are part of us. They also know immigrants are here to build, not destroy,” said Cato’s @David_J_Bier before the Senate Budget Committee. 

Read full testimony: 
ow.ly/RxPU50YtzHZ

Explore economics with Cato Courses!  

This course explores how everyday economic freedom, not heavy government control, has helped millions escape poverty and build better lives. 

Learn more by signing up for Cato Courses today. 100% free – anytime, anywhere. 

➡️ cato.courses/

The US has already spent an estimated $19 billion on the war with Iran. Here are five reasons lawmakers should hesitate in financing yet another overseas conflict, from Cato Institute experts Benjamin Giltner and @LettDominik:  

1. The administration is pursuing an undefined war with no clear exit. 
2. “Mowing the lawn” is not a strategy. 
3. Costs are already steep, and inventory is hard to replace. 
4. An emergency war supplemental is irresponsible fiscal policy. 
5. Economic power underwrite

Waiving the Jones Act would increase domestic energy movements, which would mean fewer market distortions and more competitive pricing at the margin. A full repeal would provide benefits far beyond oil and fuel. Learn more from Cato’s @cpgrabow. 

ow.ly/GSNh50YtAhK

Wisconsin prison officials violated Antonio Smith’s Eighth Amendment right against cruel and unusual punishment—but qualified immunity prevents him from getting legal relief. These constitutional violations shouldn’t go unremedied, argues a Cato brief filed with the Supreme Court. 
ow.ly/ViJ450Ytx6k

AI policy discussions increasingly hinge on control: who sets the terms for how AI can be used, what it can say, and who gets access. In a new Cato Podcast episode, @MrRBourne hosts @jrhuddles to discuss the federal government’s escalating dispute with Anthropic, New York’s proposal to police chatbot advice, and the public fears making restrictive AI policy more politically attractive. 

Listen on Apple, Spotify, and our website. 

Apple: ow.ly/523n50YtL1w  
Spotify: ow.ly/JqZA50YtL1H  
Website:

The Jones Act is a century-old law that raises costs, hurts the environment, and blocks competition—while failing to deliver on its promises. It’s time to scrap this failed policy, explains Cato's @cpgrabow.

Video

Talk of a Jones Act waiver amid rising energy and fertilizer costs tied to Middle East conflict underscores a basic problem: the law restricts shipping options and drives up transportation costs in the US, says @cpgrabow. 

If it must be waived during crises, why keep it at all? 

ow.ly/laA950YtA5h

“Immigrants come to work for us and with us to make our lives better. They have reduced government deficits by at least $14.5 trillion over the last 30 years,” testified Cato’s @David_J_Bier before the Senate Budget Committee. 

Read full testimony: 
ow.ly/PBEJ50Ytzn1

The US is the only G7 country that conducted a larger share of its total goods trade with countries within its geographical region in 2023 than it did in 2016. 

Learn more in a new research analysis by Cato’s James Bacchus ➡️ ow.ly/RXc950Yt1m0

“Given the totality of Iran’s geography, demographics, military capability, and geopolitical connections, the prospect of an American invasion of the country should be unthinkable,” writes Cato’s @brandan_buck. 

ow.ly/ESNh50YsY6K

As the US embraces tariffs, other countries are lowering trade barriers with each other by concluding new trade agreements. 

Learn more from James Bacchus as he explains world trade without the US: cato.org/policy-analysis/wor…

Video

The use of Special Operations Forces in Iran would be extraordinarily risky, operationally complex, and unlikely to accomplish its stated mission, argues the Cato Institute’s @brandan_buck. 

ow.ly/aCBy50YsYh0

“If the Jones Act must be suspended for national security emergencies, why keep it during peacetime—or at all?” asks @cpgrabow of the Cato Institute as the Trump administration reportedly weighs temporary Jones Act waivers amid Iran war-driven oil price spikes.
